sets.passage: In many ecosystems, flowering plants and the animals that pollinate them form intricate webs of interaction known as pollination networks. Rather than depending on a single partner, most plants are visited by several kinds of insects, birds, or bats, and most pollinators feed from many plant species. This overlapping structure gives the network a degree of resilience. If one pollinator disappears because of disease or habitat loss, the plants it served can often still be visited by others. Ecologists have found that these networks are rarely random. A small number of generalist species tend to interact with a great many partners, forming hubs that hold the web together. Specialists, which rely on just one or two partners, sit at the network's edges and are far more vulnerable to disruption. Understanding this architecture helps conservationists predict which species losses would ripple most widely through a community, and it guides efforts to protect the connections on which both plants and animals ultimately depend.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Pollination networks link plants and pollinators in overlapping, structured webs that affect their resilience.
Most flowering plants depend on a single specialized pollinator for reproduction.
Generalist pollinators are more likely to go extinct than specialists.
Pollination networks are formed entirely by random interactions among species.
questions.answer: Pollination networks link plants and pollinators in overlapping, structured webs that affect their resilience.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what often happens when one pollinator disappears?
options: Every plant it served immediately becomes extinct.
The plants it served can often still be visited by other pollinators.
Specialist species take over its exact role in the network.
The entire network collapses all at once.
questions.answer: The plants it served can often still be visited by other pollinators.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that specialist species are especially vulnerable because
options: they interact with a great many partners.
they form the central hubs of the network.
they depend on only one or two partners.
they feed only on birds and bats.
questions.answer: they depend on only one or two partners.

questions.prompt: The word "hubs" in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: distant edges
solid barriers
empty gaps
central connecting points
questions.answer: central connecting points

sets.explain_zh: 传粉网络将植物与传粉者连成重叠而有结构的网，通才物种构成的枢纽增强了网络的韧性。

sets.passage: Beneath most forests lies a hidden partnership between trees and fungi that has shaped plant life for millions of years. Fine fungal threads, called hyphae, wrap around or penetrate plant roots, forming an association known as mycorrhiza. The fungus extends far into the soil, reaching water and minerals such as phosphorus that the roots alone could not obtain. In return, the plant supplies the fungus with sugars produced during photosynthesis. This exchange benefits both partners so reliably that most land plants form such relationships. Recent studies suggest the fungal threads may link neighboring trees into a shared network. Through these connections, carbon and warning signals can pass from one plant to another, allowing older trees to support struggling seedlings nearby. Some scientists caution that the extent of this cooperation is still debated, and that fungi may act partly in their own interest. Even so, the discovery has transformed how researchers picture a forest, revealing an underground economy of trade rather than a collection of isolated individuals.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what does the fungus provide to the plant?
options: Sugars produced during photosynthesis
Water and minerals such as phosphorus
Protection from grazing animals
Seeds for reproduction
questions.answer: Water and minerals such as phosphorus

questions.prompt: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

Photosynthesis takes place mainly in the roots of trees.
Trees and fungi form a mutually beneficial partnership that may connect a forest.
A forest is simply a collection of completely isolated individual trees.
questions.answer: Trees and fungi form a mutually beneficial partnership that may connect a forest.

questions.prompt: The passage states that through fungal connections, trees may

produce many more flowers each spring.
grow taller than all surrounding plants.
pass carbon and warning signals to one another.
questions.answer: pass carbon and warning signals to one another.

options: To acknowledge that the extent of the cooperation is not fully understood

To show that trees cannot survive without animals
questions.answer: To acknowledge that the extent of the cooperation is not fully understood

sets.explain_zh: 菌根是树木与真菌互利共生、并可能把整片森林连成地下贸易网络的关系。

sets.passage: Every year, countless birds, sea turtles, and even insects travel enormous distances between breeding and feeding grounds, often returning to the very place where they were born. How they navigate across featureless oceans and unfamiliar terrain has long puzzled scientists. Evidence now points to several cooperating cues. Many animals read the position of the sun by day and the pattern of stars by night, adjusting for the time as it shifts. Others appear to detect Earth's magnetic field, using it as an internal compass that works even under cloudy skies. Some species also rely on landmarks, smells, or the direction of ocean swells. Because no single cue is reliable in all conditions, animals seem to combine them, switching from one to another as circumstances change. Young migrants often inherit a rough sense of direction and distance, then refine it through experience on later journeys. This layered system, built from redundant sources of information, helps explain how migrants complete journeys that would defeat a traveler equipped with a single method.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, how do some animals navigate under cloudy skies?
options: By reading the pattern of the stars
By following other migrating animals
By detecting Earth's magnetic field
By memorizing the position of the sun
questions.answer: By detecting Earth's magnetic field

questions.answer: Migrating animals rely on several cooperating cues rather than one.

young animals already know every landmark.

questions.answer: no single cue works reliably in every condition.

questions.answer: overlapping and backup

sets.explain_zh: 迁徙动物依赖太阳、星辰、地磁等多重冗余线索导航，而非单一方法。

sets.passage: Nitrogen makes up most of the air we breathe, yet plants cannot use it in its gaseous form. The nitrogen atoms are bound together so tightly that few organisms can break them apart. A group of soil bacteria, however, possesses an enzyme capable of splitting these bonds and converting nitrogen into ammonia, a form plants can absorb. Certain plants, especially legumes such as beans and clover, have formed a close alliance with these bacteria. The plant grows swollen nodules on its roots that house the microbes, supplying them with sugars and a low-oxygen environment in which their enzyme can function. In exchange, the bacteria deliver a steady supply of usable nitrogen. This partnership enriches the soil, which is why farmers have long rotated legumes with other crops to restore fertility. When the legumes die and decompose, the nitrogen they captured becomes available to plants that follow. In this way, a chemical reaction hidden inside tiny root nodules quietly sustains much of the productivity of natural and agricultural landscapes.

questions.prompt: Why can most plants not use nitrogen directly from the air?
options: The air contains too little nitrogen.
Plants prefer to absorb oxygen instead.
Nitrogen gas is poisonous to plant roots.
The nitrogen atoms are bound together too tightly.
questions.answer: The nitrogen atoms are bound together too tightly.

questions.prompt: What do legume plants provide to the bacteria in their nodules?
options: Sugars and a low-oxygen environment
Ammonia and extra water

Seeds to reproduce with
questions.answer: Sugars and a low-oxygen environment

questions.prompt: Why does the author mention crop rotation with legumes?
options: To explain how bacteria damage farmland
To illustrate a practical use of the nitrogen partnership
To argue that legumes should never be planted
To show that nitrogen is harmful to soil
questions.answer: To illustrate a practical use of the nitrogen partnership

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that when legumes decompose, they
options: remove all nitrogen from the soil.
poison nearby crops for years.
release captured nitrogen for later plants to use.

questions.answer: release captured nitrogen for later plants to use.

sets.explain_zh: 豆科植物根瘤中的细菌把空气中的氮转化为植物可用的氨，从而肥沃土壤。

sets.passage: Bats that hunt at night face a problem: they must locate small, fast-moving insects in near-total darkness. Many species solve it through echolocation, a form of biological sonar. The bat emits rapid pulses of high-pitched sound, far above the range of human hearing, and listens for the echoes that bounce back from objects around it. By measuring the tiny delay between a call and its echo, the animal judges how far away a target lies. Subtle changes in the returning sound reveal the size, texture, and even the movement of prey. As a bat closes in, it increases the rate of its calls, gathering more frequent updates during the final, critical moment of capture. Some insects have evolved countermeasures, including ears tuned to a bat's frequencies and erratic flight that is hard to track. A few moths can even produce clicks that may jam the signal. This ongoing contest between predator and prey has sharpened the abilities of both, driving remarkable refinements in hearing and flight on each side.

questions.prompt: What is the passage mainly about?
options: How bats use echolocation to hunt and how insects respond
Why bats are unable to fly in complete darkness
How human hearing compares with that of moths
Why insects are the only nocturnal animals
questions.answer: How bats use echolocation to hunt and how insects respond

questions.prompt: According to the passage, how does a bat judge the distance to its target?
options: By the color of the returning echo
By the loudness of the insect's wings
By the delay between a call and its echo
By the temperature of the surrounding air
questions.answer: By the delay between a call and its echo

questions.prompt: Why does a bat increase its call rate as it nears prey?
options: To warn other bats away from the area
To gather more frequent updates during capture
To rest its hearing between hunts
To imitate the sound of the insect
questions.answer: To gather more frequent updates during capture

sets.explain_zh: 蝙蝠用回声定位捕猎，而昆虫也进化出干扰信号或躲避的对抗手段。

sets.passage: Octopuses, squid, and cuttlefish are among the animal kingdom's most accomplished quick-change artists. Within a fraction of a second, they can transform the color and pattern of their skin to match a background, signal to a rival, or startle a predator. This ability rests on specialized skin cells. Near the surface lie thousands of tiny sacs of pigment called chromatophores, each surrounded by muscles. When the muscles pull, a sac spreads into a wide disk of color; when they relax, it shrinks to a nearly invisible dot. Beneath these cells sit reflecting layers that scatter light to create silvers, blues, and greens the pigments alone cannot make. Remarkably, the animal's brain controls this display directly, bypassing the slower chemical signals that govern color change in many other creatures. What makes the feat stranger still is that these animals are almost certainly colorblind, yet they produce patterns finely matched to their surroundings. How a colorblind animal achieves such accurate camouflage remains one of the intriguing puzzles of animal biology.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what happens when the muscles around a chromatophore pull?
options: The sac shrinks to a nearly invisible dot.
The sac spreads into a wide disk of color.
The animal loses its ability to see.
The reflecting layers stop working.
questions.answer: The sac spreads into a wide disk of color.

questions.prompt: What role do the reflecting layers play?
options: They store the animal's pigment sacs.
They send chemical signals to the brain.
They slow the animal's color change.
They create colors the pigments alone cannot make.
questions.answer: They create colors the pigments alone cannot make.

questions.prompt: The phrase "quick-change artists" in the passage is closest in meaning to

animals that paint underwater scenes

questions.answer: creatures that rapidly alter their appearance

sets.explain_zh: 头足类靠色素细胞和反光层快速变色伪装，尽管它们几乎是色盲。

sets.passage: Young songbirds are not born knowing the songs of their species; many must learn them, much as human infants learn speech. A nestling first listens to the singing of adult males around it, storing a memory of the pattern long before it can sing itself. Months later, the young bird begins to practice, producing rambling, imperfect sounds that scientists compare to the babbling of a baby. Gradually it adjusts its output, matching what it produces against the memorized model until the two align. This process depends on a sensitive period early in life. If a bird hears no adult song during that window, it will later sing only a crude, abnormal version, and the deficit cannot be fully repaired afterward. Regional populations of the same species often develop distinctive local dialects, passed from one generation to the next through learning rather than inheritance. Because the stages of this learning resemble those of human language so closely, songbirds have become valuable models for researchers investigating how brains acquire complex, patterned behavior of any kind.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what does a nestling do first?
options: It practices rambling, imperfect sounds.
It develops a distinctive local dialect.
It listens to and memorizes adult songs.
It teaches songs to other birds.
questions.answer: It listens to and memorizes adult songs.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that a bird isolated from adult song during the sensitive period will
options: sing only a crude, abnormal version later.
learn songs perfectly as an adult.
invent an entirely new species song.
lose the ability to fly.
questions.answer: sing only a crude, abnormal version later.

options: Songbirds are born already knowing their species' songs.

sets.passage: When winter reduces the food supply, some mammals survive by entering hibernation, a state far more profound than ordinary sleep. In a hibernating ground squirrel, the heart may slow from hundreds of beats per minute to only a few, and body temperature can fall close to that of the surrounding den. In this condition the animal burns stored fat extremely slowly, stretching limited energy reserves across many months. Yet hibernation is not a single, unbroken rest. Every week or two, the animal briefly warms itself back to a normal temperature, an expensive process that consumes a large share of its saved energy. Why it does so remains uncertain; some researchers suspect these arousals allow the body to carry out repairs or restore immune defenses that cold suspends. Preparing for this ordeal takes months. Through late summer and autumn, the animal eats heavily, laying down the thick fat layer that will fuel it until spring. Understanding how hibernators protect their tissues during such extreme cold has even inspired medical research on preserving human organs.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what happens to a hibernating ground squirrel's heart rate?
options: It rises to hundreds of beats per minute.
It stays exactly the same as when awake.
It stops entirely for several months.
It slows to only a few beats per minute.
questions.answer: It slows to only a few beats per minute.

consume a large share of the animal's saved energy.

questions.answer: consume a large share of the animal's saved energy.

sets.passage: Living cells depend on thousands of chemical reactions, yet at body temperature most of these reactions would proceed far too slowly to sustain life. Enzymes solve this problem. An enzyme is a protein that acts as a catalyst, speeding up a specific reaction without being used up in the process. It works by lowering the energy barrier that reacting molecules must overcome, so that a change which might otherwise take hours occurs in a fraction of a second. Each enzyme has a distinctive folded shape, and part of that shape forms a pocket called the active site. Only molecules that fit this pocket, like a key entering a lock, can be acted upon, which is why enzymes are so precise about the reactions they assist. Their activity is sensitive to conditions. Excessive heat or the wrong level of acidity can distort the delicate shape, causing the enzyme to lose its function, sometimes permanently. Because a cell can produce or withhold particular enzymes, it can switch whole chemical pathways on or off, controlling its own internal chemistry with remarkable precision.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Enzymes speed up specific cell reactions and are governed by their shape and conditions.
Chemical reactions in cells cannot be sped up at all.
Enzymes are used up quickly during each reaction.

questions.answer: Enzymes speed up specific cell reactions and are governed by their shape and conditions.

questions.prompt: How does an enzyme speed up a reaction?
options: By raising the body's overall temperature
By becoming part of the final product
By adding extra energy to the molecules
By lowering the energy barrier the molecules must overcome
questions.answer: By lowering the energy barrier the molecules must overcome

questions.prompt: The word "distort" in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: strengthen
twist out of shape
clean thoroughly
produce more of
questions.answer: twist out of shape

questions.prompt: The lock-and-key comparison is used to explain why enzymes are

questions.answer: precise about which reactions they assist.

sets.explain_zh: 酶作为催化剂通过降低能垒并凭借特定形状精确地加速细胞内的化学反应。

sets.passage: Because plants cannot flee from the animals that eat them, many have evolved chemical defenses instead. Their tissues may contain bitter or toxic compounds that discourage feeding, damage an insect's digestive system, or slow its growth. Some of these substances are produced constantly, while others appear only after an attack begins. When a caterpillar starts chewing a leaf, the wounded plant can rapidly manufacture defensive chemicals at the damaged site, and sometimes throughout its whole body. More surprising still, certain plants release airborne compounds when injured. Neighboring plants that detect these signals may begin preparing their own defenses before they are touched, as if forewarned. The same airborne signals can attract predatory insects that attack the very caterpillars feeding on the plant, turning the plant's enemies into targets for its allies. Producing these compounds costs energy that might otherwise support growth or reproduction, so plants tend to invest in defense most heavily when the threat is greatest. This flexible, layered chemistry reveals a hidden and surprisingly active side of organisms often assumed to be passive.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what may some plants do when a caterpillar begins chewing?
options: Move away from the caterpillar.
Rapidly manufacture defensive chemicals at the damaged site.
Stop producing all chemicals immediately.

questions.answer: Rapidly manufacture defensive chemicals at the damaged site.

questions.answer: Warn neighboring plants to prepare their defenses.

sets.explain_zh: 植物无法逃跑，便以化学物质防御，甚至释放气味警示邻株或引来天敌。

sets.passage: Wetlands cover only a small fraction of the planet's land surface, yet they store a disproportionate share of its carbon. In waterlogged soils, oxygen is scarce, and the microbes that normally break down dead plants work slowly or not at all. As a result, leaves, roots, and stems accumulate faster than they decay, piling up over centuries into thick, dark layers of peat. Locked within this material is carbon that plants originally drew from the atmosphere, kept out of the air as long as the ground stays wet. Peatlands that took thousands of years to form can therefore hold enormous quantities of carbon in a relatively small area. The danger comes when wetlands are drained for farming or fuel. Exposed to air, the stored plant matter begins to decompose or burn, releasing its carbon as gases that warm the climate. Protecting and restoring wetlands is thus increasingly seen not merely as a way to shelter wildlife, but as a practical strategy for keeping vast reserves of carbon safely out of the atmosphere.

questions.prompt: Why do dead plants accumulate rather than decay in wetlands?
options: The plants are simply too tough to break down.
Microbes there eat only living plants.
Waterlogged soils lack the oxygen microbes need.
The climate is always too cold for decay.
questions.answer: Waterlogged soils lack the oxygen microbes need.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what happens when wetlands are drained?
options: Peat begins to form much more quickly.
Stored plant matter decomposes and releases carbon.
The carbon becomes permanently locked away.
Wildlife populations immediately increase.
questions.answer: Stored plant matter decomposes and releases carbon.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Wetlands store large amounts of carbon that is released if they are drained.
Wetlands cover most of the planet's land surface.
Peat is a poor material for storing carbon.
Draining wetlands has no effect on the climate.
questions.answer: Wetlands store large amounts of carbon that is released if they are drained.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that protecting wetlands helps the climate because it

removes oxygen from the atmosphere.

questions.answer: keeps stored carbon from entering the air.

sets.explain_zh: 湿地缺氧使植物残体堆积成泥炭储存大量碳，排干湿地会释放这些碳加剧变暖。

sets.passage: As human activities release carbon dioxide into the atmosphere, the oceans absorb a large portion of it. This absorption slows the warming of the air, but it changes the chemistry of seawater. When carbon dioxide dissolves, it reacts with water to form a weak acid, gradually lowering the ocean's pH in a process called ocean acidification. Since the industrial era began, surface waters have become measurably more acidic than before. The change poses a particular threat to organisms that build shells or skeletons from calcium carbonate, including corals, oysters, and many tiny floating creatures at the base of the food web. More acidic water makes the mineral harder to form and can even dissolve existing structures. Because these small organisms feed vast numbers of larger animals, disruption near the bottom of the food chain may ripple upward through entire ecosystems. Acidification also often occurs alongside warming, and the two stresses together may prove harder to withstand than either alone. Scientists therefore monitor ocean chemistry closely, treating it as an early warning of wider ecological change.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what forms when carbon dioxide dissolves in seawater?
options: A harmless gas that escapes into the air
A layer of solid mineral on the surface
Extra oxygen for marine animals
A weak acid that lowers the ocean's pH
questions.answer: A weak acid that lowers the ocean's pH

questions.prompt: Why are shell-building organisms especially threatened?
options: They cannot survive in warm water at all.
They depend entirely on sunlight for food.
Acidic water makes calcium carbonate harder to form.
They are simply too small to be seen.
questions.answer: Acidic water makes calcium carbonate harder to form.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that damage to small shell-building organisms matters because
options: they produce most of the ocean's carbon dioxide.
they feed vast numbers of larger animals.
they are the only source of ocean acidity.
they prevent the ocean from ever warming.
questions.answer: they feed vast numbers of larger animals.

questions.prompt: Why does the author mention that acidification often occurs alongside warming?
options: To point out that the combined stresses may be harder to withstand
To argue that warming cancels out acidification

To show that carbon dioxide is harmless
questions.answer: To point out that the combined stresses may be harder to withstand

sets.explain_zh: 海洋吸收二氧化碳变酸，威胁造壳生物并可能自下而上波及整个生态系统。

sets.passage: Cities are often several degrees warmer than the countryside around them, a phenomenon known as the urban heat island. The difference arises largely from the materials that cover urban surfaces. Asphalt, concrete, and brick absorb sunlight during the day and store its heat, then release it slowly through the evening, keeping streets warm long after sunset. Tall buildings add to the effect by trapping heat within narrow spaces and blocking breezes that might carry it away. Meanwhile, cities contain far less vegetation than rural areas. Trees and grass cool their surroundings by shading the ground and releasing water vapor, and where they are replaced by pavement, that natural cooling is lost. Vehicles, factories, and air conditioners add still more heat directly. The consequences reach beyond mere discomfort: higher temperatures increase energy demand for cooling and can worsen air quality and heat-related illness. Planners have found that relatively simple measures, such as planting trees, creating parks, and using lighter-colored roofing that reflects sunlight, can meaningfully reduce the warming and make dense neighborhoods more livable.

questions.prompt: What is the passage mainly about?
options: Why cities are warmer than surrounding areas and how the effect can be reduced
Why the countryside is warmer than nearby cities
How air conditioners cool an entire city
Why planting trees makes cities hotter
questions.answer: Why cities are warmer than surrounding areas and how the effect can be reduced

questions.prompt: According to the passage, how do trees and grass cool their surroundings?
options: By absorbing and storing heat like concrete
By shading the ground and releasing water vapor
By trapping heat within narrow spaces
By blocking cooling breezes
questions.answer: By shading the ground and releasing water vapor

questions.prompt: Which measure does the passage mention for reducing urban heat?
options: Building taller and narrower structures
Replacing parks with pavement
Adding more vehicles and factories

questions.answer: Using lighter-colored roofing that reflects sunlight

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that dark pavement contributes to the heat island because it

releases large amounts of water vapor.

questions.answer: absorbs sunlight and stores heat.

sets.explain_zh: 城市因深色硬化地面吸热、植被减少而比郊区更热，可通过绿化和浅色屋顶缓解。

sets.passage: When a species is carried, deliberately or by accident, into a region where it never lived before, the results are unpredictable. Most newcomers fail to survive, but a few thrive spectacularly, becoming what ecologists call invasive species. Freed from the predators, diseases, and competitors that once held them in check in their native range, such organisms may multiply rapidly and spread across the new landscape. As they do, they can crowd out native plants and animals, consume them directly, or alter the habitat in ways that make it unsuitable for the original residents. A vine that blankets and smothers native trees, or a fish that devours the young of local species, can reshape an entire community within a few years. The economic costs are also large, as invaders damage crops, clog waterways, and force expensive control efforts. Removing an established invader is notoriously difficult and sometimes impossible, so specialists increasingly stress prevention: inspecting cargo, regulating trade in living organisms, and acting quickly when a new arrival is first detected, before it can establish itself firmly.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why can some introduced species multiply so rapidly?
options: They are carefully protected by local laws.
They escape the predators and diseases of their native range.
They can reproduce only in cold climates.
They depend on native trees for all their food.
questions.answer: They escape the predators and diseases of their native range.

questions.prompt: What does the passage give as an example of an invader's impact?
options: A tree that shelters native birds
A fish that cleans local waterways
A vine that smothers native trees
A bird that pollinates native flowers
questions.answer: A vine that smothers native trees

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Invasive species can cause severe harm, so prevention is emphasized.

sets.explain_zh: 外来入侵种因摆脱天敌而泛滥，危害本地生态与经济，故防范胜于清除。

sets.passage: For much of the twentieth century, land managers treated every wildfire as a disaster to be extinguished as quickly as possible. Ecologists have since learned that fire is a natural and often necessary part of many landscapes. In certain forests and grasslands, periodic low-intensity fires clear away dead material, return nutrients to the soil, and open space for young plants to grow. Some species depend on fire directly: a number of pines, for instance, bear cones sealed with resin that melts only in intense heat, releasing their seeds onto freshly cleared ground. Suppressing fire in these places can backfire. Without regular small burns, dead branches and dense undergrowth accumulate year after year, forming a thick layer of fuel. When a fire finally does ignite, it can grow far larger and hotter than the blazes the ecosystem evolved with, destroying even the mature trees that would normally survive. Recognizing this, managers now sometimes set carefully controlled fires under safe conditions, deliberately burning small amounts of fuel to prevent the catastrophic fires that decades of suppression can unintentionally create.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what do periodic low-intensity fires do in some forests?
options: They permanently destroy the soil.
They prevent all plants from growing.
They clear dead material and return nutrients to the soil.
They permanently seal pine cones with resin.
questions.answer: They clear dead material and return nutrients to the soil.

questions.prompt: How do certain pines depend on fire?
options: Their roots grow only in fresh ash.
Their leaves need smoke in order to survive.
Their bark repels all flames completely.
Their cones release seeds only when heat melts the resin.
questions.answer: Their cones release seeds only when heat melts the resin.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that decades of fire suppression can lead to larger fires because
options: young plants crowd out the mature trees.
dead fuel accumulates and feeds bigger blazes.
the soil loses all of its nutrients.
pines stop producing any cones.
questions.answer: dead fuel accumulates and feeds bigger blazes.

questions.prompt: The passage as a whole argues that
options: fire can be a natural, necessary process that suppression may worsen.
all wildfires should be extinguished immediately.
controlled fires are more dangerous than wildfires.
forests never recover after any fire.
questions.answer: fire can be a natural, necessary process that suppression may worsen.

sets.explain_zh: 火在许多生态系统中是必要过程，一味灭火反而积累燃料酿成更大火灾。

sets.passage: Although water covers most of the Earth's surface, only a tiny fraction is fresh and available for human use. Much of that supply lies hidden underground in aquifers, layers of porous rock and sediment that hold water in their spaces. People reach it by drilling wells, and in many dry regions such groundwater is the main source of drinking water and irrigation. Aquifers are refilled slowly as rain and melting snow seep down through the soil, a process that may take years, centuries, or longer. Trouble arises when water is withdrawn faster than it is replaced. Water tables fall, wells must be drilled deeper, and the ground itself can sink as the emptied spaces collapse. Along coasts, over-pumping may draw salty seawater into the aquifer, spoiling the freshwater it once held. Because some large aquifers were filled during wetter climates thousands of years ago, the water they contain is, in practical terms, nonrenewable. Managing these hidden reserves wisely has become essential in regions where they support agriculture and cities that could not otherwise exist.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, how are aquifers refilled?
options: By pumping water back down through wells
By seawater flowing in from the coast
By rivers draining directly into the wells
By rain and melting snow seeping through the soil
questions.answer: By rain and melting snow seeping through the soil

questions.prompt: What can happen when water is withdrawn faster than it is replaced?
options: The ground can sink and water tables fall.
The aquifer refills much more quickly.
The water becomes purer over time.
Rainfall in the region increases sharply.
questions.answer: The ground can sink and water tables fall.

questions.prompt: The word "porous" in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: solid and sealed
extremely heavy
full of tiny spaces
brightly colored
questions.answer: full of tiny spaces

questions.prompt: The passage suggests that some large aquifers are "nonrenewable" because
options: they contain only salt water.
they were filled long ago and refill too slowly to replace heavy use.

questions.answer: they were filled long ago and refill too slowly to replace heavy use.

sets.explain_zh: 地下含水层补给缓慢，过度抽取会导致地面下沉、海水入侵，需谨慎管理。

sets.passage: Wind turbines convert the motion of moving air into electricity, offering a source of power that produces no fuel costs and no carbon emissions while running. As the blades turn, they spin a generator, and the stronger and steadier the wind, the more energy is produced. For this reason, turbines are often placed on open plains, along coastlines, or offshore, where winds blow with fewer obstructions. Yet wind power presents a distinctive challenge: it is intermittent. The wind does not blow on demand, and output can swing from hour to hour, whereas electricity must be supplied to match consumption at every moment. Operators address this in several ways. They spread turbines across wide areas so that calm conditions in one place may be offset by breezes in another, and they combine wind with other sources that can be adjusted quickly. Increasingly, they also store surplus electricity, in batteries or by other means, for release when the air is still. As such balancing methods improve, wind is able to supply a steadily growing share of many regions' power.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Wind power is clean but intermittent, and operators use several methods to balance its variable output.
Wind turbines are the only source of clean electricity.
Wind power is far too unreliable ever to be used.
Electricity demand stays exactly constant all day.
questions.answer: Wind power is clean but intermittent, and operators use several methods to balance its variable output.

questions.prompt: Why are turbines often placed offshore or on open plains?
options: Because electricity is cheaper to store there
Because the land is otherwise useless
Because winds blow with fewer obstructions there
Because carbon emissions are lower at sea
questions.answer: Because winds blow with fewer obstructions there

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why is matching wind output to demand difficult?
options: Turbines produce too much fuel.
Electricity cannot be generated at night.
Demand rises only in the summer.
The wind does not blow on demand.
questions.answer: The wind does not blow on demand.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that spreading turbines across wide areas helps because

it makes the wind blow more steadily everywhere.
questions.answer: calm in one place may be offset by wind elsewhere.

sets.explain_zh: 风电清洁但具间歇性，运营商通过分散布点、多源互补和储能来平衡波动。

sets.passage: Soil forms slowly, sometimes at a rate of only a few centimeters over a thousand years, yet it can be lost with alarming speed. When land is plowed and left bare, wind and rain carry away the fine, fertile top layer far faster than natural processes can rebuild it. This top layer holds most of the organic matter and nutrients that crops depend on, so its loss steadily reduces the land's productivity. Erosion also carries soil into rivers and lakes, where it clouds the water and smothers aquatic life. Farmers and scientists have developed methods to slow these losses. Planting cover crops keeps roots in the ground between harvests, binding the soil in place. Plowing along the contours of a slope, rather than up and down it, reduces the speed of running water. Leaving crop residue on the surface shields the soil from pounding rain. Such practices, taken together, can conserve in a single season the fertility that would otherwise take many human lifetimes to replace, protecting a resource on which food production ultimately rests.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why is the loss of topsoil especially serious?
options: It makes the soil form far more quickly.
It holds most of the nutrients that crops depend on.
It contains no organic matter at all.
It protects nearby rivers from pollution.
questions.answer: It holds most of the nutrients that crops depend on.

questions.prompt: How does plowing along the contours of a slope help?
options: It makes new soil form much faster.
It increases the amount of bare ground.
It carries more soil into the rivers.
It reduces the speed of running water.
questions.answer: It reduces the speed of running water.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Soil forms slowly but erodes quickly, and farming methods can conserve it.
Soil is best protected by leaving fields completely bare.
Erosion actually improves the fertility of farmland.
Rivers are the main source of new soil.
questions.answer: Soil forms slowly but erodes quickly, and farming methods can conserve it.

bind the soil and hold it in place.

sets.explain_zh: 表土形成极慢却易流失，覆盖作物、等高耕作等措施可有效保土保肥。

sets.passage: For most of history, nights were genuinely dark, and living things evolved to depend on that darkness. The spread of artificial lighting has changed the nighttime environment across much of the world, producing what scientists call light pollution. Its effects reach well beyond the inconvenience of a sky too bright to reveal the stars. Many animals rely on natural light cues to guide essential behavior. Newly hatched sea turtles, for example, instinctively crawl toward the brightest horizon, which over open beaches is the sea; artificial lights inland can lure them the wrong way, to their deaths. Migrating birds that navigate by the stars may become disoriented by illuminated buildings and circle them until exhausted. Nocturnal insects gather helplessly around lamps, disrupting the feeding of the predators and pollinators that depend on them. Even plants and humans, whose internal rhythms are tuned to the cycle of day and night, can be affected by constant light. Encouragingly, this form of pollution is unusually easy to reverse: shielding lamps, dimming unnecessary lights, and switching them off restores darkness almost at once.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why do artificial lights endanger newly hatched sea turtles?
options: The lights make the sand around them too hot.
The turtles are attracted to loud noises.
The turtles crawl toward the brightest horizon and are lured inland.
The lights frighten the turtles into the sea.
questions.answer: The turtles crawl toward the brightest horizon and are lured inland.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Artificial light disrupts many organisms, but the problem is easy to reverse.
Light pollution mainly affects our view of the stars.
Nocturnal insects benefit greatly from artificial lamps.
Darkness is harmful to most living things.
questions.answer: Artificial light disrupts many organisms, but the problem is easy to reverse.

questions.prompt: How does light pollution affect migrating birds?
options: It improves their overall sense of direction.
It can disorient them and make them circle buildings.
It causes them to migrate earlier each year.
It has no measurable effect on them at all.
questions.answer: It can disorient them and make them circle buildings.

questions.prompt: Why does the author end by describing shielding and dimming lamps?
options: To argue that all outdoor lighting should be banned
To prove that light pollution can never be fixed
To suggest that darkness itself harms wildlife
To emphasize that this pollution is unusually easy to reverse
questions.answer: To emphasize that this pollution is unusually easy to reverse

sets.explain_zh: 人造光扰乱海龟、候鸟和昆虫等生物的自然节律，但关灯遮光即可迅速逆转。

sets.passage: Plastic waste that reaches the environment does not simply disappear. Exposed to sunlight, waves, and friction, larger items gradually break into ever smaller fragments, until they become particles less than five millimeters across, known as microplastics. Others enter the environment already small, such as fibers shed from synthetic clothing during washing. Because they are so tiny and so durable, microplastics have spread almost everywhere scientists have looked, from deep ocean sediments to mountain snow and the bodies of animals. Small creatures at the base of the food web may swallow the particles, mistaking them for food, and the plastics can then pass to the larger animals that eat them. Researchers worry not only about physical harm but also about the chemicals plastics may carry, some of which cling to their surfaces from the surrounding water. Much about the long-term consequences remains uncertain, and studying particles so small and widespread is difficult. What is clear is that because microplastics are nearly impossible to remove once dispersed, reducing the plastic that enters the environment in the first place is the most practical response.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, how do most microplastics form?
options: They are manufactured at exactly that size for industry.
They condense directly out of ocean water.
They grow larger over time in sunlight.
Larger plastic items break into ever smaller fragments.
questions.answer: Larger plastic items break into ever smaller fragments.

questions.prompt: How can microplastics move up the food web?
options: By dissolving completely into the seawater
Small creatures swallow them and pass them to larger animals.
By evaporating into mountain snow
By turning back into large plastic items
questions.answer: Small creatures swallow them and pass them to larger animals.

questions.prompt: Why does the author note that chemicals "cling" to microplastics from the surrounding water?

To suggest an additional way microplastics may be harmful
To argue that the chemicals involved are always safe
questions.answer: To suggest an additional way microplastics may be harmful

questions.answer: Reducing the plastic that enters the environment in the first place

sets.explain_zh: 塑料碎裂成微塑料后几乎无处不在且难以清除，从源头减少塑料才最实际。

sets.passage: Volcanic eruptions are among nature's most destructive events, yet over time they can give rise to some of the world's most fertile land. When a volcano erupts, it hurls out ash: fine particles of pulverized rock rich in minerals such as potassium, phosphorus, and magnesium. In the immediate aftermath, thick ash can bury crops and poison water, causing hardship for anyone living nearby. But as the ash weathers and mixes with the soil over years and decades, those same minerals are released in forms that plants can absorb. Soils derived from volcanic material are often loose and porous as well, holding moisture while still allowing roots to breathe. For these reasons, the slopes around many active volcanoes support unusually productive farmland, and dense human populations have long settled there despite the obvious risks. This creates a lasting tension. The very geological activity that renews the soil and draws people to the land is also capable, without warning, of destroying the communities that depend on it, a bargain that has shaped life in volcanic regions for thousands of years.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Volcanoes can create fertile soil, drawing people despite the danger they pose.
Volcanic ash is completely useless for growing crops.
People almost never settle near active volcanoes.
Eruptions improve the soil instantly after they occur.
questions.answer: Volcanoes can create fertile soil, drawing people despite the danger they pose.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what makes volcanic soils good for plant roots?
options: They contain no minerals at all.
They are hard and tightly packed.
They are loose and porous, holding moisture while letting roots breathe.

questions.answer: They are loose and porous, holding moisture while letting roots breathe.

questions.prompt: What does the passage say happens in the immediate aftermath of an eruption?

questions.answer: Thick ash can bury crops and poison water.

questions.prompt: The "lasting tension" the author describes refers to the fact that volcanic activity

sets.explain_zh: 火山灰风化后释放矿物质造就肥沃土壤，吸引人们定居却也带来毁灭风险。

sets.passage: Each year, most trees in temperate climates add a new layer of wood beneath their bark, forming the rings visible when a trunk is cut across. In spring, rapid growth produces wide, pale wood; later in the season, growth slows and the wood becomes darker and denser. Together these make one annual ring, so counting the rings reveals a tree's age. The rings hold more than a simple tally, however. Their width records the conditions of each growing season: a wet, favorable year leaves a broad ring, while drought or cold leaves a narrow one. Because trees in the same region experience the same weather, their ring patterns match, and scientists can overlap samples from living trees with older wood from ancient timbers to build continuous records reaching back thousands of years. Such records have helped date archaeological sites, reconstruct past climates, and even reveal the timing of long-ago volcanic eruptions and droughts. In this way, an ordinary tree quietly keeps a detailed diary of its environment, one that patient researchers have learned to read line by line.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what does the width of a tree ring record?
options: The exact age of the tree in days
The conditions of each growing season
The direction the tree was facing
The number of branches the tree grew
questions.answer: The conditions of each growing season

questions.prompt: How is a single annual ring formed?
options: By pale spring wood and darker, denser later wood together
By a single layer of bark added each winter
By the roots pushing upward once a year
By falling leaves staining the trunk
questions.answer: By pale spring wood and darker, denser later wood together

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that overlapping samples from living trees and ancient timbers allows scientists to
options: make trees grow much faster.
predict next year's weather exactly.
extend climate records back thousands of years.
determine a tree's height from its rings.
questions.answer: extend climate records back thousands of years.

questions.prompt: Why does the author describe a tree as keeping "a detailed diary of its environment"?
options: To argue that trees are able to write.
To suggest that trees remember specific people.

To emphasize that rings preserve a record of past conditions.
questions.answer: To emphasize that rings preserve a record of past conditions.

sets.explain_zh: 树木年轮宽窄记录各年气候，通过样本叠合可重建数千年的环境与历史记录。

sets.passage: A glacier may look like a motionless mass of ice, but it is in fact a slow-moving river of frozen water. Where snow falls faster than it melts, layer builds upon layer, and the weight of the accumulating snow compresses the lower layers into dense ice. Once the ice grows thick enough, gravity sets it in motion, and it begins to flow downhill or spread outward under its own weight. This movement occurs in two main ways. Deep within the glacier, the ice deforms and slides internally, one layer creeping past another under enormous pressure. Nearer the base, a thin film of meltwater can lubricate the boundary between ice and rock, allowing the whole glacier to slide forward. As it travels, the ice grinds against the land, plucking loose boulders and scraping the bedrock, carving out the broad valleys and sharp ridges that glaciers leave behind. The speed of this journey is usually measured in meters per year, imperceptible to a watching eye, yet over long spans of time glaciers have reshaped entire mountain ranges and continents.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what first sets a glacier in motion?
options: A sudden rise in the air temperature
Strong winds pushing against the ice
Gravity acting on ice that has grown thick enough
Rivers flowing beneath the snow
questions.answer: Gravity acting on ice that has grown thick enough

questions.prompt: How does meltwater near the base affect a glacier?
options: It freezes the glacier firmly in place.
It lubricates the boundary so the glacier can slide.
It causes the glacier to melt completely.
It stops the ice from deforming internally.
questions.answer: It lubricates the boundary so the glacier can slide.

questions.prompt: What does a glacier do as it travels across the land?
options: It smooths the bedrock into flat plains only.
It deposits fresh snow on the mountains.
It leaves the landscape completely unchanged.
It grinds against the land, carving valleys and ridges.
questions.answer: It grinds against the land, carving valleys and ridges.

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that glaciers reshape mountains despite moving slowly because
options: they act over very long spans of time.
they travel many kilometers each day.
they are made of rock rather than ice.
they melt and refreeze every single night.
questions.answer: they act over very long spans of time.

sets.explain_zh: 冰川在重力下缓慢流动，靠内部变形和底部融水滑动，长期侵蚀塑造山川地貌。

sets.passage: Some of the most spectacular caves on Earth are carved not by rushing rivers but by slow chemistry. They form in limestone, a rock made largely of calcium carbonate. Rainwater, as it falls and seeps through soil, absorbs carbon dioxide and becomes slightly acidic. Over thousands of years, this weak acid trickles through cracks in the limestone and gradually dissolves the rock, widening tiny fissures into passages and eventually into vast underground chambers. The process does not end when the cave is hollow. Water dripping from a cave ceiling carries dissolved minerals, and as each drop lingers and loses its carbon dioxide, a trace of calcium carbonate is left behind. Drop by drop, over ages, these deposits build downward into icicle-like stalactites hanging from above and upward into stalagmites rising from the floor. Where the two meet, they form a continuous column. Because the mineral layers accumulate at a steady, measurable pace, scientists can read them almost like tree rings, extracting from a single stalactite a record of ancient rainfall and climate stretching back tens of thousands of years.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, how does rainwater become able to dissolve limestone?
options: By heating up sharply as it falls
By freezing solid inside the cracks
By mixing with nearby river water
By absorbing carbon dioxide and becoming slightly acidic
questions.answer: By absorbing carbon dioxide and becoming slightly acidic

questions.prompt: How does a stalactite form?
options: Rushing rivers pile up sand on the ceiling.
Acid eats a hole straight through the rock.
Dripping water leaves behind traces of calcium carbonate.
Wind deposits fine dust on the cave floor.
questions.answer: Dripping water leaves behind traces of calcium carbonate.

questions.prompt: The word "fissures" in the passage is closest in meaning to
options: narrow cracks
large rooms
smooth surfaces
flowing streams
questions.answer: narrow cracks

questions.prompt: Why does the author compare stalactite layers to tree rings?
options: To argue that caves contain living trees.
To show that both preserve a dated record of past climate.
To prove that stalactites can grow leaves.
To suggest that trees form inside caves.
questions.answer: To show that both preserve a dated record of past climate.

sets.explain_zh: 弱酸性雨水溶蚀石灰岩形成溶洞，滴水沉积的钟乳石还记录了古气候。

sets.passage: Where a river meets a still body of water, such as a sea or a lake, it suddenly slows. Throughout its course the moving water has carried a load of sediment, from fine mud to coarse sand, but slow water cannot hold these particles, so they settle out. Layer by layer, over centuries, the deposited material builds a low, often fan-shaped landform called a delta. As sediment accumulates, the river frequently splits into a branching network of smaller channels that wander across the growing surface, shifting course as some passages clog and new ones open. Deltas are among the most fertile places on Earth, for each flood spreads a fresh layer of nutrient-rich silt across the land, and great civilizations have flourished on them. Yet they are also fragile. A delta survives only while the river continues to deliver enough sediment to offset the natural sinking and erosion of its soft ground. When dams upstream trap sediment, or when rising seas advance, the balance can tip, and land that took thousands of years to build may be lost within a few generations.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why does a river deposit its sediment where it meets the sea?
options: The water slows and can no longer hold the particles.
The water suddenly speeds up at the coast.
The sediment dissolves in the salt water.
The river freezes when it reaches the coast.
questions.answer: The water slows and can no longer hold the particles.

questions.prompt: What happens to a river as sediment accumulates in a delta?
options: It flows backward toward its source.
It splits into a branching network of smaller channels.
It stops carrying any sediment at all.
It carves out a single deep canyon.
questions.answer: It splits into a branching network of smaller channels.

questions.prompt: Why are deltas considered fertile?
options: Because the water there is unusually salty
Because they almost never flood
Because floods spread fresh, nutrient-rich silt
Because dams reliably supply them with sediment
questions.answer: Because floods spread fresh, nutrient-rich silt

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that dams upstream can threaten a delta because they
options: cause the river to flood far more often.
add extra sediment to the delta.
make the local sea level fall.
trap sediment the delta needs to rebuild itself.
questions.answer: trap sediment the delta needs to rebuild itself.

sets.explain_zh: 河流入海减速沉积泥沙形成肥沃三角洲，但上游筑坝截沙或海平面上升会使其消失。

sets.passage: The Earth's rigid outer shell is not a single unbroken layer but a set of enormous plates that fit together like pieces of a cracked shell. These plates rest on hotter, slowly flowing rock beneath them and drift a few centimeters each year, about as fast as a fingernail grows. Most dramatic geological events happen at their boundaries. Where two plates pull apart, usually along ridges on the ocean floor, molten rock rises from below, cools, and hardens into new crust, gradually widening the ocean between them. Where plates collide, one may bend and plunge beneath the other into the depths, or the two may crumple upward, raising mountain ranges over millions of years. Where plates grind sideways past each other, the built-up strain is released in sudden jolts we feel as earthquakes. The idea that continents move was once dismissed, since no one could explain what force could shift them. The discovery that the seafloor itself spreads outward from mid-ocean ridges finally provided the missing mechanism and transformed our understanding of how the planet's surface is continually remade.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what happens where two plates pull apart?
options: One plate plunges beneath the other.
Molten rock rises and hardens into new crust.
Mountain ranges are pushed upward.
The plates grind sideways past each other.
questions.answer: Molten rock rises and hardens into new crust.

questions.prompt: What does the passage say causes earthquakes at some boundaries?
options: Molten rock cooling on the ocean floor
New crust forming at the ridges
Strain released as plates grind sideways past each other
The slow upward growth of mountain ranges
questions.answer: Strain released as plates grind sideways past each other

questions.prompt: How fast do the plates drift, according to the passage?
options: Several meters each day
Hundreds of kilometers a year
Only during large earthquakes
A few centimeters each year, about as fast as a fingernail grows
questions.answer: A few centimeters each year, about as fast as a fingernail grows

questions.prompt: Why does the author mention that continental movement "was once dismissed"?
options: To highlight that seafloor spreading supplied the missing explanation
To prove that continents do not actually move
To argue that earthquakes are impossible to predict
To show that oceans never change in size
questions.answer: To highlight that seafloor spreading supplied the missing explanation

sets.explain_zh: 地球板块缓慢漂移，其边界处的张裂、碰撞和错动造就了新地壳、山脉和地震。

sets.passage: The oceans are in constant motion, and some of their currents form a vast, connected system that moves heat around the globe. Near the surface, winds drive great streams of warm water from the tropics toward the poles, warming the air above coastal lands along the way. As this water travels into cold regions, two things happen: it loses heat to the atmosphere, and, where sea ice forms, it grows saltier, because ice leaves its salt behind. Cold, salty water is dense, and dense water sinks. In a few key locations, surface water plunges to the deep ocean and begins a slow return journey along the bottom, eventually rising again elsewhere to complete the loop. This overturning circulation, driven by differences in temperature and saltiness, may take a thousand years to finish a single cycle. Its influence is enormous, for it helps regulate regional climates and distributes nutrients through the sea. Scientists watch it closely because they suspect that a large influx of fresh water from melting ice could weaken this circulation, with consequences felt far beyond the ocean itself.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, why does surface water sink in certain cold regions?
options: Because winds physically push it downward
Because it becomes warmer and lighter
Because it becomes cold and salty, and therefore dense
Because dissolved nutrients weigh it down
questions.answer: Because it becomes cold and salty, and therefore dense

questions.prompt: How does the formation of sea ice affect the remaining water?
options: It makes the water saltier by leaving salt behind.
It removes all the salt from the water.
It warms the surrounding water.
It speeds the water back toward the tropics.
questions.answer: It makes the water saltier by leaving salt behind.

questions.prompt: About how long may a single cycle of this circulation take?
options: A few days
About a thousand years
Exactly one year
A single season
questions.answer: About a thousand years

questions.prompt: It can be inferred that melting ice could weaken the circulation because it would
options: make the ocean much warmer overall.

sets.passage: Sand dunes are sculpted wherever loose sand, wind, and open space come together. The wind lifts and rolls individual grains across the surface, and when moving air meets an obstacle, such as a shrub or a slight rise, it slows and drops part of its load. Sand begins to pile up, and the growing mound itself becomes an obstacle that traps still more. A dune typically develops two distinct faces. On the windward side, the gentler slope, the wind pushes sand upward toward the crest. On the far, steeper side, sheltered from the wind, the grains tumble down and settle. Because sand is continually removed from one side and deposited on the other, the whole dune slowly migrates downwind, sometimes advancing several meters in a year and burying whatever lies in its path. The shape a dune takes depends on the supply of sand, the strength of the wind, and the vegetation present. Where plants take root, their tangled roots can anchor the sand and halt the dune's march.

questions.answer: The wind slows when it meets an obstacle.

questions.answer: sand is removed from one side and deposited on the other.

sets.passage: Fossils are the preserved traces of ancient life, yet the vast majority of organisms that ever lived left none behind. Preservation requires an unusual chain of events. When most creatures die, scavengers, bacteria, and weather quickly break their remains apart. A body stands a chance of becoming a fossil only if it is buried rapidly, often by mud or sand at the bottom of a lake or sea, before it can be destroyed. Soft tissues usually rot away regardless, so the hard parts, bones, shells, and teeth, are what typically survive. As sediment piles up and hardens into rock over long ages, minerals dissolved in groundwater may seep into the buried remains and slowly replace the original material, turning it to stone while preserving its shape in fine detail. Because these conditions are rare, the fossil record is uneven and incomplete, favoring hard-bodied animals living in watery settings over soft or land-dwelling ones. Scientists must therefore read this partial archive with care, aware that it offers not a full census of past life but a series of fortunate glimpses into it.

questions.answer: It must be buried rapidly before it is destroyed.

sets.passage: A geyser is a rare kind of hot spring that erupts, throwing a column of boiling water and steam high into the air at intervals. Only a few places on Earth have the special combination of conditions needed to produce one. First, there must be a source of underground heat, usually molten rock lying relatively close to the surface. Second, there must be an abundant supply of groundwater. Third, and most crucial, the underground plumbing must include a narrow, twisting channel that can trap water and let pressure build. Water deep in this channel is heated far above the normal boiling point, yet the weight of the water above presses down and keeps it from boiling. Eventually some of it flashes into steam, pushing water out at the top. This lightens the load below, the pressure suddenly drops, and the superheated water erupts violently. Afterward the channel refills, the water reheats, and the cycle repeats, sometimes with remarkable regularity. Because the required conditions are so particular, the world's major geyser fields are few, clustered in regions where volcanic heat lies near the surface.

questions.answer: A narrow, twisting channel that traps water and lets pressure build

questions.answer: The weight of the water above presses down on it.

questions.answer: Some water flashes into steam and pushes water out the top.

sets.passage: On steep hillsides, farming communities have long faced a persistent problem: rain washes away both soil and seed before crops can take root. Terracing offers one enduring solution. By cutting a slope into a staircase of level platforms, each supported by a low retaining wall, farmers slow the downhill flow of water and trap fertile sediment that would otherwise be lost. The flat surfaces hold moisture long enough for roots to establish, and the walls reduce the force of sudden storms. Building terraces demands enormous labor, and maintaining them demands more, since a single collapsed wall can send debris cascading onto the fields below. For this reason, terraced landscapes usually reflect generations of shared effort rather than the work of any single season. Where communities abandoned the practice, hillsides often eroded within decades, stripping away soil that had taken centuries to accumulate. The endurance of terraces thus depended less on their initial construction than on the steady, coordinated upkeep that kept them functioning.

sets.passage: In many pre-industrial towns, craft guilds controlled who could practice a trade and how goods were made. A guild was more than a social club; it was a regulatory body that set standards for materials, workmanship, and price. A weaver or metalworker who wished to sell in the town market usually had to belong to the relevant guild, and the guild inspected finished products for defects. This oversight served several purposes at once. It protected customers from shoddy goods, protected honest artisans from being undercut by cheaper rivals, and protected the town's reputation, since buyers from elsewhere associated quality with the place of origin. Critics have long noted the cost of this system: by limiting the number of practitioners, guilds kept prices high and slowed the spread of new techniques that might threaten established members. Yet the same restrictions that discouraged innovation also preserved skills, funded training, and guaranteed a level of reliability that unregulated markets rarely achieved on their own.

sets.passage: Many early societies measured time by watching the moon, whose phases repeat about every twenty-nine and a half days. A calendar of twelve such months, however, runs to roughly three hundred fifty-four days, some eleven days shorter than the solar year that governs the seasons. Left uncorrected, a purely lunar calendar drifts steadily against the agricultural cycle, so that a festival meant for the harvest gradually slides into spring and then into winter. To keep their months aligned with the seasons, various cultures inserted an extra month every few years, a practice known as intercalation. Deciding exactly when to add this leap month required careful observation and often fell to priests or officials, giving those who controlled the calendar considerable authority. Other societies eventually abandoned the moon altogether and built calendars purely on the solar year, accepting months that no longer matched the lunar phases. The tension between the moon's rhythm and the sun's was thus resolved differently in different places, but almost never ignored.

sets.passage: Long-distance overland trade depended on more than brave merchants and sturdy pack animals; it required a network of places to stop. Along major routes, travelers found caravanserais, roadside inns spaced roughly a day's journey apart. A typical caravanserai enclosed a large courtyard behind high walls, with stalls for animals on the ground floor and rooms for people above. Within these walls, merchants could rest, water their beasts, repair equipment, and store goods safely overnight before setting out again at dawn. The regular spacing of such inns was not accidental. Because a loaded caravan could cover only a limited distance between sunrise and sunset, stations too far apart would have left travelers exposed to weather and robbery, while stations too close together would have been unnecessary and costly to maintain. Beyond shelter, caravanserais functioned as centers of exchange, where traders from different regions met, compared prices, and passed along news. In this way the humble roadside inn quietly shaped the rhythm and reach of overland commerce.

sets.passage: Before standardized coins, people who wanted to trade using precious metal faced a tedious problem. Every transaction required weighing lumps of gold or silver and testing their purity, since a piece might be mixed with cheaper metals. This slowed exchange and invited disputes. The invention of coinage addressed both difficulties at once. A coin was a piece of metal stamped by an issuing authority, and the stamp served as a guarantee of weight and purity. In principle, a trader could now accept a coin by counting rather than weighing, trusting the authority behind the mark. This convenience helps explain why coined money spread rapidly once introduced. Yet the guarantee was only as trustworthy as the issuer. Rulers short of funds sometimes reduced the precious-metal content of their coins while keeping the same face value, a practice that could quietly erode public confidence. Coinage therefore did not remove the need for trust; it relocated that trust from the metal itself to the institution whose stamp the metal bore.

sets.passage: As early states grew, they confronted a problem of memory. A ruler could remember a promise or a debt, but a kingdom of thousands could not rely on any single person's recollection. Written records offered a solution, and with them came the archive: a place where documents were stored, organized, and retrieved. Tax receipts, land grants, court judgments, and treaties were copied and filed so that decisions made in one year could be consulted in the next. An archive did more than preserve information; it shifted authority from living memory to a durable, checkable record. A claim to own land no longer rested solely on what neighbors remembered but could be tested against a filed document. This made large-scale administration possible, but it also concentrated power in the hands of those who could read the records and control access to them. Ordinary people, unable to verify what the archive contained, often had to trust officials. The archive thus became both a tool of order and a source of quiet authority.

sets.passage: In dry regions, irrigation canals could turn arid land into productive fields, but canals posed a challenge that individual farmers could not solve alone. A canal must be cleared of silt, its banks repaired, and its water shared according to some agreed schedule. If one farmer near the source took more than a fair share, those downstream received too little; if no one dredged the channel, it filled with mud and served no one. Because the benefits and burdens fell on many households at once, maintaining a canal required rules and a way of enforcing them. Communities developed detailed customs specifying how much labor each household owed to repairs and how water was to be rotated among fields. Disputes were common, and settling them fairly was essential, since a system perceived as unjust would collapse as farmers stopped contributing. The canal, in other words, was as much a social institution as an engineering work. Its water flowed only as long as the cooperation that sustained it held together.

sets.passage: Harvests are seasonal, but hunger is not. A community that gathered all its grain in autumn still needed to eat through the following spring, and a single failed harvest could bring famine. Granaries, buildings designed to store surplus grain safely, allowed societies to bridge these gaps. Good storage was harder than it sounds. Grain had to be kept dry, protected from rodents and insects, and turned or ventilated to prevent rot, so granary design reflected hard-won practical knowledge. When states took charge of large public granaries, storage became a tool of policy as well as survival. Rulers could release stored grain in years of scarcity to prevent starvation and unrest, or lend it to farmers who had exhausted their own supplies. Controlling the granary therefore meant controlling a powerful lever over the population. Some governments used this power to stabilize prices, buying grain when it was cheap and selling when it grew dear. In such systems, the granary was not merely a warehouse but an instrument of governance.

sets.passage: A large empire faced a problem of speed. A messenger carrying an official dispatch would ride hard to the next station, hand over the message or exchange his tired mount for a rested one, and the message would continue with barely a pause. By relaying messages from station to station, such systems achieved speeds no single rider or animal could sustain. These networks were reserved for official business, since maintaining them was expensive and their value lay in reliability. A ruler who could learn quickly of distant events, and respond before they spread, held a decisive advantage over rivals dependent on rumor. The relay network was thus less a convenience than a foundation of effective central control.

sets.passage: In many past societies, laws regulated not only what people could do but what they could wear. Known as sumptuary laws, these rules restricted certain fabrics, colors, or ornaments to particular ranks. A dye that was rare and costly, for example, might be reserved by law for the highest officials, so that a glance at a person's clothing revealed their place in the social order. Such laws reflected an anxiety about mobility. As trade enriched merchants who lacked noble birth, the wealthy but low-ranking could afford garments once limited to elites, blurring the visible line between classes. Sumptuary laws attempted to freeze appearances so that dress continued to signal status reliably. In practice, these rules were difficult to enforce and were frequently broken, and the very need to legislate against imitation suggests that imitation was widespread. Over time, as economies grew more commercial, most such laws faded. Their history reveals how seriously societies once took the idea that outward appearance should correspond to inward rank.

sets.passage: The earliest writing systems did not begin with letters standing for sounds. Many started with pictures, in which a simple drawing of an object represented that object directly. Such picture-signs worked well for concrete things but struggled with abstract ideas and grammar. Over time, scribes found that a sign could stand not for a thing but for the sound of the word that named it. Once signs represented sounds, they could be combined to spell words that no picture could easily capture, including names and abstract concepts. The number of signs needed, however, remained large, and mastering them took years. A further step reduced signs to represent individual consonants or syllables, shrinking the inventory a learner had to memorize. Alphabets, in which a small set of signs represents basic sounds, made literacy easier to acquire, though they did not automatically spread it, since access to schooling remained limited. The history of writing is thus not a single invention but a long series of shifts from representing things toward representing sounds.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Writing evolved from representing objects toward representing sounds, through several stages.
Alphabets were invented before picture-signs.
Literacy became universal as soon as alphabets appeared.

sets.passage: Before writing was widespread, long poems were composed and preserved by memory alone, and performers could recite works of great length without a written text. Scholars once found this astonishing, until they noticed a distinctive feature of such poetry: its heavy use of repeated phrases. A hero might be described again and again with the same fixed epithet, and whole lines or scenes might recur almost word for word. These repeated formulas were not signs of a poor imagination. They were tools of memory and composition. A performer working without a text could draw on a stock of ready-made phrases that fit the rhythm of the verse, assembling and adjusting them as he went rather than recalling every word exactly. This helps explain why oral poems vary from one performance to another: each telling is partly rebuilt on the spot. Far from being a fixed object like a printed book, an oral poem was a living tradition, reshaped by every performer who carried it forward while keeping its essential story intact.

sets.passage: A museum can seem to offer a neutral window onto the past, its objects simply there to be seen. Yet every display is the result of choices, and those choices shape what visitors understand. Curators decide which objects to show and which to leave in storage, how to arrange them, and what labels to attach. An ancient tool placed in a glass case under careful lighting invites quiet admiration; the same tool shown alongside the materials it once processed invites thoughts about daily labor. The words on a label matter too, since a caption can present an object as a masterpiece, as evidence of trade, or as a symbol of conquest. None of these framings is false, but none is the whole story, and each directs attention in a particular direction. Recognizing this does not mean museums deceive their visitors. It means that a display is an argument as much as a presentation, and that thoughtful visitors gain by asking not only what an object is but why it is shown in this particular way.

sets.passage: A folk song has no single fixed version. Unlike a composition printed in a score, a traditional song passes from singer to singer by ear, and each singer may alter it, sometimes deliberately and sometimes without noticing. A melody might be simplified, a verse forgotten, or a new stanza added to suit a local occasion. As a result, collectors who travel from village to village often record many variants of what is recognizably the same song. Scholars once treated these differences as errors, imagining a single original that later singers had corrupted. A different view now prevails. Variation is not decay but the very way the tradition lives, since a song survives precisely because singers feel free to adapt it to their voices and circumstances. A version that could never change would be a museum piece, learned but not truly used. The many forms of a folk song are therefore best seen not as departures from a lost perfect original but as evidence of a tradition actively passed from one generation to the next.

sets.passage: When a work of literature crosses into another language, it does not arrive unchanged. A translator must make countless decisions, because words rarely map neatly from one language to another. A single term may carry associations, sounds, or double meanings that no equivalent in the target language reproduces exactly. The translator must therefore choose what to preserve and what to sacrifice, and different translators, facing the same passage, may choose differently. As a result, a foreign author can be known in a new country through a particular translation, and readers' impression of that author is shaped by choices they never see. A translation that favors smooth, natural phrasing may make a difficult writer seem easy; one that stays close to the original's strangeness may make an accessible writer seem forbidding. Neither is simply wrong. Over generations, works are often retranslated, and each new version reflects not only the original text but the tastes and assumptions of its own time. To read a translated classic is thus to read the original at one remove, through a lens the translator has ground.
